agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "A global logistics platform adding real-time driver earnings transparency across three jurisdictions with fundamentally different labor classifications (employee vs. independent contractor) and regulatory regimes. The feature must present earnings breakdowns (base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, deductions) while respecting jurisdiction-specific legal constraints on compensation display.",
    "bounded_context": "Driver Compensation / Earnings Visibility",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Base Pay", "definition": "The guaranteed minimum compensation for a delivery or time period, before bonuses, tips, or deductions. For employees (CA, DE): hourly/shift wage. For contractors (ID): per-task base rate." },
      { "term": "Per-Delivery Bonus", "definition": "Variable compensation tied to completing a specific delivery, such as surge pricing, distance premium, or difficulty modifier." },
      { "term": "Tip", "definition": "Voluntary gratuity from the customer passed through to the driver. Must never be counted toward base pay in employee jurisdictions." },
      { "term": "Deduction (Employee)", "definition": "Mandatory or authorized withholdings from gross pay: taxes, social insurance, benefits contributions. Applies in CA and DE where drivers are employees." },
      { "term": "Platform Fee (Contractor)", "definition": "Service charges or commission retained by the platform from a contractor's gross earnings. Applies in ID. Must NOT be labeled 'deduction' as that implies an employment relationship." },
      { "term": "Earnings Breakdown", "definition": "The itemized view of all compensation components for a given period, presented to the driver." },
      { "term": "Works Council Approval (Betriebsrat)", "definition": "Formal consent from the German employee representative body, legally required before any change to compensation display systems can be deployed in Germany." },
      { "term": "Driver Classification", "definition": "The legal labor status of the driver — employee or independent contractor — which governs applicable wage laws, display requirements, and terminology." },
      { "term": "Pay Period", "definition": "The time window over which earnings are aggregated. Varies by jurisdiction and classification." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Displayed earnings must exactly reconcile with actual payments issued to the driver. The sum of all displayed line items must equal the net pay the driver receives.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "If displayed earnings diverge from actual payments, the system is producing false financial records. This is data corruption — it erodes trust, creates legal liability, and violates basic accounting integrity.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any driver and any pay period: SUM(base_pay + bonuses + tips - deductions_or_fees) == net_payment_issued, with zero discrepancy."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Compensation terminology and line-item labels must align with the driver's legal classification in their jurisdiction. Employee jurisdictions must use payroll language (wages, deductions). Contractor jurisdictions must use commercial language (earnings, platform fees). Mislabeling creates reclassification risk.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Using employee terminology for contractors (or vice versa) constitutes evidence of misclassification in legal proceedings. This is not a cosmetic choice — it is a legal exposure that could force reclassification of an entire workforce in a jurisdiction.",
      "testable_assertion": "A driver in Indonesia never sees the term 'deduction', 'wage', or 'withholding'. A driver in California never sees the term 'platform fee' or 'commission'."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "In California, the full earnings breakdown (base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, and all deductions with itemization) must be displayed to every driver. This is not optional — it is legally mandated under wage transparency requirements.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "California wage transparency law and AB5 employee classification make this a legal compliance requirement. Failure to display is a statutory violation, not a policy choice.",
      "testable_assertion": "Every California driver's earnings view contains at minimum: base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, and an itemized deductions section. No line item is hidden or collapsed by default."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "The Germany deployment is gated on documented Works Council (Betriebsrat) approval. The feature must not be visible to any German driver until this approval is recorded in the system.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "German co-determination law (Betriebsverfassungsgesetz §87) gives works councils mandatory co-determination rights over compensation display systems. Deploying without approval is illegal and exposes the company to injunctions and fines.",
      "testable_assertion": "If betriebsrat_approval_status != 'approved', the earnings breakdown feature returns HTTP 404 or is feature-flagged off for all drivers with jurisdiction = 'DE'."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Tips must never be counted toward or offset against base pay in employee jurisdictions (California, Germany).",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Tip credit against minimum wage is prohibited in California. Using tips to meet base pay obligations constitutes wage theft — a legal violation, not a policy choice.",
      "testable_assertion": "Base pay value is identical whether or not tips exist for the same delivery. Removing all tips from a pay period does not change the base pay line item."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "In California, deductions must be itemized by category (federal tax, state tax, Social Security, Medicare, disability insurance, etc.). A single aggregated 'deductions' line is insufficient.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The level of itemization granularity is a compliance policy that could be adjusted based on legal counsel's interpretation of California Labor Code requirements. The requirement to show deductions is invariant (BR-3), but the specific granularity is policy.",
      "testable_assertion": "California earnings breakdown shows at least 3 distinct deduction line items, not a single 'Total Deductions' row."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "In Indonesia, the platform may choose whether to display the earnings breakdown or not, since local law does not require pay transparency. However, if displayed, it must use contractor-appropriate terminology (BR-2).",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Displaying earnings in Indonesia is a business decision, not a legal mandate. A VP could decide to enable or disable it based on business strategy.",
      "testable_assertion": "If the feature is enabled for Indonesia, no employee-specific terminology appears. If disabled, the earnings breakdown endpoint returns a graceful 'not available' response."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "German earnings display must comply with Lohnabrechnung (payslip) standards, including social insurance contributions (Sozialversicherung), income tax (Lohnsteuer), and church tax (Kirchensteuer) where applicable.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The specific line items are governed by German payroll law but the exact display format is subject to Works Council negotiation and could be adjusted through that process.",
      "testable_assertion": "German driver earnings view includes at minimum: Bruttolohn (gross pay), Lohnsteuer (income tax), Sozialversicherung (social insurance), and Nettolohn (net pay)."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A driver completes a delivery in California",
      "when": "They view their earnings breakdown",
      "then": "They see base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, and itemized deductions (taxes, insurance, etc.), all using employee-appropriate terminology, and the sum reconciles to their net pay"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "The Germany Works Council has NOT granted approval",
      "when": "A German driver attempts to access the earnings breakdown feature",
      "then": "The feature is unavailable — no earnings breakdown is displayed, and the driver sees no partial or placeholder compensation data"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "The Germany Works Council HAS granted documented approval",
      "when": "A German driver views their earnings breakdown",
      "then": "They see gross pay, itemized statutory deductions (Lohnsteuer, Sozialversicherung, Kirchensteuer if applicable), and net pay using German payroll terminology"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A driver completes a delivery in Indonesia",
      "when": "They view their earnings breakdown (if feature is enabled for ID)",
      "then": "They see base earnings, per-delivery bonus, tips, and platform fees — with no employment terminology such as 'wage', 'deduction', or 'withholding'"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A driver in any jurisdiction receives a tip of $10 on a delivery",
      "when": "The earnings breakdown is calculated",
      "then": "The base pay amount is unchanged compared to the same delivery without a tip — tips are a separate additive line item"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A driver views their earnings breakdown for any completed delivery",
      "when": "They add up all displayed line items (earnings minus deductions/fees)",
      "then": "The total exactly matches the net amount deposited to their account for that period, with zero discrepancy"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "A driver's jurisdiction is determined by the system",
      "when": "The earnings breakdown UI is rendered",
      "then": "All labels, line items, and terminology are driven by jurisdiction-specific configuration — no hardcoded assumptions about classification"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Driver completes a delivery that crosses jurisdiction boundaries (e.g., starts in one region, delivers in another)", "expected_behavior": "Earnings are attributed to a single governing jurisdiction based on platform policy (e.g., pickup location or driver's home jurisdiction). Must not produce a hybrid breakdown mixing employee and contractor terminology." },
    { "scenario": "Works Council approval is revoked or suspended mid-rollout in Germany", "expected_behavior": "Feature must be immediately disabled for all German drivers. Any cached earnings views must not remain accessible. The gate check must be evaluated on every request, not cached." },
    { "scenario": "A delivery has $0 base pay but a non-zero tip (e.g., cancelled delivery where tip was already processed)", "expected_behavior": "System must still display all line items. In California, $0 base pay on a worked delivery may trigger minimum wage violation alerts. The breakdown must not suppress zero-value lines." },
    { "scenario": "Platform fee in Indonesia exceeds the gross earnings for a delivery (negative net pay)", "expected_behavior": "The breakdown must still display accurately. A negative net amount must be shown transparently, not hidden or rolled into the next period without the driver's visibility." },
    { "scenario": "Driver relocates from Indonesia (contractor) to California (employee) mid-pay-period", "expected_behavior": "Historical earnings in Indonesia retain contractor terminology. New earnings in California use employee terminology. The system must not retroactively relabel Indonesian earnings as employee wages." },
    { "scenario": "Real-time display shows estimated earnings before payroll finalization", "expected_behavior": "Estimates must be clearly labeled as provisional/estimated. Final breakdown replaces estimates after payroll runs. California law requires accurate pay statements — estimates must not be presented as final." },
    { "scenario": "German driver's Kirchensteuer (church tax) status is unknown or not provided", "expected_behavior": "Church tax line item should be omitted rather than showing zero, since showing €0.00 Kirchensteuer implies the driver opted out, which may not be accurate. The system should prompt for missing tax data." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Non-compliance fines in California (Labor Code violations: $100-$250 per pay period per employee). German works council disputes can trigger injunctions halting operations. Misclassification in Indonesia could trigger retroactive reclassification and back-pay liability. Correct implementation reduces wage dispute resolution costs.",
    "operational": "Requires jurisdiction-aware configuration system rather than a single global template. Germany rollout is blocked on an external approval process with unpredictable timeline. Payroll systems must feed real-time data to the display layer, increasing integration complexity. Each jurisdiction needs independent feature flags.",
    "user": "Drivers gain trust and satisfaction through pay transparency — industry research shows transparent pay reduces driver churn by 10-15%. California drivers receive a legally mandated right. Indonesian drivers get optional visibility that differentiates the platform from competitors.",
    "risk": "HIGH in California (statutory violation risk). HIGH in Germany (works council injunction risk). MEDIUM in Indonesia (misclassification terminology risk). Cross-jurisdiction complexity increases the probability of bugs that mix classification contexts. A single UI bug showing 'deductions' to Indonesian contractors could be cited as evidence of misclassification in future litigation.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Drivers (California)", "impact": "Receive legally mandated earnings transparency. Empowered to verify correct pay. Improved trust and retention." },
      { "group": "Drivers (Germany)", "impact": "Feature blocked until Works Council approves. May experience delayed rollout. Must see compliant Lohnabrechnung-style breakdown." },
      { "group": "Drivers (Indonesia)", "impact": "Optional feature. Must never see employment-implying language that could affect their legal classification." },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance Team", "impact": "Must validate terminology per jurisdiction. Must obtain and document Works Council approval. Ongoing monitoring for regulatory changes." },
      { "group": "Betriebsrat (German Works Council)", "impact": "Has co-determination right. Must be consulted and must approve before any German deployment. May negotiate display format and content." },
      { "group": "Payroll/Finance", "impact": "Must provide real-time accurate data feed. Displayed amounts become auditable records. Discrepancies between display and payment create liability." },
      { "group": "Product/Engineering", "impact": "Must build jurisdiction-aware, configurable system. Cannot use one-size-fits-all approach. Feature flag infrastructure required per jurisdiction." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 52,
    "reasoning": "The spec is underspecified in critical areas: (1) 'real time' is undefined — it could mean per-delivery, per-shift, or streaming, each with very different technical and legal implications; (2) 'deductions' is used as a single term across three jurisdictions where it means fundamentally different things; (3) no mention of how Works Council approval is tracked or gated in the system; (4) the legal requirements cited (AB5, Betriebsrat) are real but the spec does not indicate whether legal counsel has validated the specific display requirements. Two ambiguity escalations drive confidence below 55.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Driver classification alignment with jurisdiction is non-negotiable",
      "Works Council approval is a hard legal gate for Germany",
      "Tips must not offset base pay in employee jurisdictions",
      "Displayed earnings must reconcile with actual payments"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Definition of 'real time' — per-delivery vs. per-shift vs. streaming",
      "Exact deduction line items required per jurisdiction without legal counsel input",
      "Whether Indonesia feature should be enabled by default or opt-in",
      "How cross-border deliveries should be jurisdictionally attributed"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Driver jurisdiction is determined by a single authoritative source (e.g., employment contract location or registration country), not by delivery location",
      "The payroll system can provide near-real-time data accurate enough for display purposes",
      "Legal counsel has confirmed AB5 applies to this platform's drivers in California",
      "The German entity has an established Works Council — if not, the Betriebsrat gate may not apply"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The term 'deductions' is used uniformly in the spec but has fundamentally different legal and domain meanings across jurisdictions. For California employees, deductions are payroll withholdings (taxes, insurance). For Indonesian contractors, the equivalent concept is platform fees/commission — calling them 'deductions' implies an employment relationship and creates misclassification risk. The spec must explicitly define jurisdiction-specific line items and terminology.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance Team", "Drivers (Indonesia)", "Product/Engineering"],
      "options": [
        "A: Define a jurisdiction-specific terminology map as part of the spec, with legal review per jurisdiction",
        "B: Use a generic term like 'adjustments' globally and let each jurisdiction configure sub-labels",
        "C: Treat this as three separate features with independent specs per jurisdiction"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A — a single feature with a jurisdiction-driven terminology configuration. This preserves code reuse while enforcing legal accuracy. Each jurisdiction gets a reviewed label set."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "'Real time' is undefined. In a payroll context, this could mean: (a) updated after each delivery completion, (b) updated at end of shift, (c) streaming/live during a delivery. California's pay statement laws require accuracy — showing estimates labeled as final amounts is a violation. The definition of 'real time' directly affects data accuracy guarantees and legal compliance.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers (all jurisdictions)", "Payroll/Finance", "Legal/Compliance Team"],
      "options": [
        "A: Define 'real time' as post-delivery — breakdown updates after each completed delivery with finalized amounts",
        "B: Define 'real time' as end-of-day — aggregate view updated daily",
        "C: Define 'real time' as streaming estimates with a clear 'estimated' label, replaced by final amounts after payroll"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A — post-delivery update with finalized per-delivery amounts. This is the safest interpretation: it meets driver expectations for immediacy while ensuring amounts are final (not estimated), satisfying California's accuracy requirements."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No information on how Works Council approval status is tracked, stored, or verified in the system. Is it a manual flag set by legal? An integration with an approval workflow? How is revocation handled? This is a hard deployment gate — the implementation cannot be designed without knowing the approval lifecycle.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Betriebsrat", "Legal/Compliance Team", "Product/Engineering"],
      "options": [
        "A: Simple boolean feature flag managed by legal team manually",
        "B: Formal approval workflow with audit trail, approval date, approver identity, and revocation capability",
        "C: Integration with existing Works Council communication/approval system if one exists"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B — a formal approval record with audit trail. Given the legal weight of this gate, a simple boolean is insufficient for compliance audits. The system needs to prove when approval was granted and by whom."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
